claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 5 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.**

**How old is the child?**

Please tell me which category applies:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children, we can go through each one. Just let me know the age(s) of the child(ren) you'd like to claim for.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm looking to claim for my son Alex. He's only 5 years old, so definitely under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Since Alex is 5 years old and under 16, that's a great start - children under 16 automatically meet the age requirement for Child Benefit.

Let me continue to the next step of the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

Since Alex is only 5 years old, I imagine he's not working - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, he's not working at all - he's just a little one!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! At 5 years old, Alex definitely isn't working, so that's no problem at all. Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in his own right?**

This means benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit that would be claimed by Alex himself, rather than by you as his parent.

For a 5-year-old, this would almost certainly be "no" - he wouldn't be receiving these benefits in his own name. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct - he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's what I expected for a young child. Let's continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

**Is Alex in an apprenticeship?**

At 5 years old, I'm confident Alex isn't in any kind of apprenticeship. Is that correct?

(Just for your information: certain apprenticeships can affect eligibility for older children, but this clearly doesn't apply to a young child like Alex!)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's definitely not in an apprenticeship - he's far too young for that!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely! Let's move on to the next part of the assessment, which will be about you as the person claiming.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now we need to check your residency status.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **any one** of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. **You have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **You have pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3+ continuous months)
4. **You have pre-settled status** and are actively seeking work (can claim for up to 91 days)
5. **You have pre-settled status** with sufficient resources to support yourself and family (and not claiming certain income-based benefits)
6. **You have pre-settled status** and are studying with sufficient resources
7. **You have pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with the right to reside
8. **You're a Crown servant** living abroad (or in certain qualifying countries)

Do any of these apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I normally live in the UK - I live in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Living in the UK meets the residency requirement perfectly. Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You qualify if **any one** of the following applies:

1. **You live with Alex**
2. **You contribute to Alex's upkeep** - paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent) towards his care. This includes things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing, or presents.
3. **You pay maintenance** for Alex under a court order or agreement

Does any of these apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Wonderful! Living with Alex clearly establishes your responsibility for him. Let's continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

This could be another parent, a partner, or another family member. 

- If **no one else** is claiming for Alex, that's straightforward.
- If **someone else is claiming** but you've both agreed that you should be the one to claim instead, that's also fine.
- If there's a **dispute** about who should claim, HMRC will need to make the final decision.

What's your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's straightforward. Let's move on to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

**Where does Alex currently live?**

I need to check if any special circumstances apply. Is Alex:

- **In hospital** (for an extended stay)?
- **In local authority care**?
- **Being fostered**?
- **Living normally at home with you**?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's living normally at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Let's continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final question:**

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, the council isn't contributing financially towards housing or looking after Alex. This would typically only apply if the council was involved in supporting Alex's care in some way.

Is that correct - the council isn't paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - the council isn't paying anything towards his accommodation or maintenance.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! That was the final check, and I'm pleased to tell you that you appear to be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Good News! You're Eligible for Child Benefit for Alex

Based on our assessment, you meet all the eligibility requirements to claim Child Benefit for your son Alex.

### Summary of Why You're Eligible:
- ✅ Alex is under 16 years old
- ✅ He's not working
- ✅ He doesn't receive benefits in his own right
- ✅ He's not in an apprenticeship
- ✅ You normally live in the UK
- ✅ You live with Alex and are responsible for him
- ✅ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him
- ✅ The council isn't paying for his accommodation or maintenance

### Next Steps to Claim:
1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** (or adoption certificate if applicable)

### Important to Know:
- Payments are usually made **every 4 weeks** (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)
- ⚠️ If you or your partner earn over **£60,000**, you may be affected by the **High Income Child Benefit Charge** - you can still claim, but some or all of it may need to be repaid through tax

### Useful Link:
🔗 [How to Claim Child Benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex meets all eligibility criteria for Child Benefit: (1) He is under 16 years old (age 5), which automatically qualifies him for the age requirement. (2) He is not working (under 24 hours per week). (3) He does not receive any benefits in his own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit). (4) He is not in any apprenticeship. (5) The claimant (parent) normally lives in the UK (Manchester), meeting residency requirements. (6) The claimant lives with Alex, establishing responsibility for the child. (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. (8) Alex lives normally at home (not in hospital, care, or fostered). (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 5-year-old son Alex. All eligibility criteria have been met: the child qualifies based on age, the claimant meets UK residency requirements, has responsibility for the child by living with him, there are no competing claims, and no disqualifying circumstances apply.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)
